title: Daily Briefing — Monday, June 8
tldr: Dev play of the day: put a hard dollar cap on your AI bill. Cloudflare's AI Gateway just shipped spend limits — set a real budget per model, app, team or user, and when it's hit, requests either stop or quietly fail over to a cheaper fallback model so nobody's workflow breaks. Open beta, every plan, ~20 minutes to wire up.

News — the market is repricing the AI trade. A soft AI-chip forecast from Broadcom (it guided ~$16B vs the ~$17.2B Street wanted, stock −12%) triggered a rotation out of AI names: on Jun 8 in Asia, SoftBank fell ~6%, Samsung ~10%, SK Hynix ~8%, dragging the Kospi down as much as 8%. The takeaway for us isn't to trade it — it's that AI spend is now under a microscope, which is exactly why today's dev play matters.

Growth — new research says AI answer engines cite some formats far more than others. Two 2026 datasets (HubSpot's State of AEO + Wix Studio's AI Search Lab, 1M+ citations) found only three formats clear a 65% citation rate: product/landing pages (68.5%), blog posts (~67%) and listicles (66%) — and pages win citations when they pair an intent-matched title (What is X / X vs Y / How to X / Best X) with original stats, a visible last-updated date, and an FAQ. Reformat one money page today.

Also building (news): fusion startup Helion raised a $465M Series G at a $15.5B valuation (nearly 3× its last round) to commercialize fusion this decade — the clearest sign yet that the bottleneck behind the AI trade is power, not just chips.

Sharpen your edge: the throughline across all three — the market is now grading AI spend, not just AI ambition. Govern your own before a platform (or a budget review) does it for you: cap the bill, and make every page you publish earn its citation.

sections.h: Web dev — run it today: a hard dollar cap on your AI spend

blocks.sub: Cloudflare AI Gateway now enforces budgets in dollars, not just request counts
blocks.p: What changed (Jun 5): Cloudflare's AI Gateway added spend limits — cost-based budgets that track real cumulative dollar spend (computed from each model's token pricing) and act when you cross the line. This is different from rate limiting: rate limits cap how many requests; spend limits cap how much money. Why it matters for our builds: the #1 fear shipping anything LLM-backed — a chatbot, a RAG feature, an agent — is a runaway loop or a traffic spike quietly burning four figures overnight. A real ceiling turns 'we daren't ship AI to production' into 'ship it, it can't cost more than $X.' It lands the same week the market is punishing loose AI spend (see the news beat) — controlling your unit economics is suddenly a feature, not an afterthought.
blocks.doIt: Wire it up in ~20 minutes: (1) route your app's LLM calls through an AI Gateway endpoint (you proxy your OpenAI/Anthropic/etc. calls through Cloudflare); (2) in the gateway's settings (dashboard or API) add a spend limit and scope it — by model, provider, or a custom attribute like user/team/app (e.g. $2,000/mo for senior engineers, $500/mo for everyone else, or $50/day on one expensive model); (3) pick a window — daily/weekly/monthly, fixed (resets 1st of month / Monday / midnight) or rolling; (4) choose the over-limit behavior: block by default, or use a Dynamic Route to fail over to a cheaper fallback model so a hard cap doesn't kill the workflow. Start with a generous monthly cap on your highest-cost model and tighten from real numbers.
blocks.note: It's open beta on all plans as of Jun 5. Treat the cost figures as Cloudflare's own pricing math — reconcile against your provider's invoice for the first month before you trust the cap to the dollar.

sections.h: News — the AI trade gets repriced; the bottleneck is power
sections.intro: Trust-critical domain — figures cross-checked against a 2nd source and marked directional where fast-moving.

blocks.sub: A soft Broadcom forecast rotated money out of AI names
blocks.p: What happened: Broadcom's Q2 FY26 print was strong on paper — revenue ~$22.2B, up ~48% YoY on AI demand — but its forward AI-semiconductor guide of ~$16B came in under the ~$17.2B analysts wanted, and the stock fell ~12% after hours (reported Jun 3). That miss-on-expectations, plus 'higher-for-longer' rate worries after a hot U.S. jobs print, sparked a rotation out of AI-linked equities. By Jun 8 in Asia it had spread: SoftBank −~6%, Samsung Electronics −~10%, SK Hynix −~8%, with chip-heavy Korea's Kospi down as much as ~8% (those two names are 40%+ of the index). Why it matters to us: not as a trading tip — as a climate signal. The era of 'spend anything on AI and the market cheers' is pausing; budgets, unit economics and provable ROI are back in fashion. That reframes how clients will scrutinize the AI features we build and pitch this quarter.
blocks.note: Directional: intraday index/stock moves shift hour to hour. The durable fact is the rotation and the why (a guidance miss + rates), corroborated across CNBC and the Broadcom earnings coverage — not any single percentage.

blocks.sub: Where the money still flooded in: power for compute (Helion's $465M)
blocks.p: Counter-signal, same week: fusion-energy startup Helion raised a $465M Series G at a $15.5B post-money valuation (nearly 3× its prior round; ~$1.5B raised to date), led by Thrive Capital, to push toward commercial fusion this decade — its Polaris prototype became the first privately funded machine to run on deuterium-tritium fuel. Read alongside the week's other infra bets (PhysicsX's $300M Series C at $2.4B for AI-designed hardware; Nvidia–SK Hynix and Nvidia–LG memory/data-center pacts), the throughline is clear: investors are cooling on AI application valuations while still pouring into the physical bottleneck — power and memory. The compute story is becoming an energy story.
blocks.doIt: Operator takeaway: when you scope an AI feature for a client this quarter, lead with cost-per-outcome and a spend ceiling (see today's Cloudflare play), not raw capability — that's the framing a repricing market now rewards, and it's what will get budget approved.

sections.h: Growth — run it today: format a page so AI answer engines actually cite it

blocks.sub: New research on 1M+ AI citations shows which formats win — and the recipe to copy
blocks.p: What changed: two independent 2026 studies — HubSpot's State of AEO 2026 and Wix Studio's AI Search Lab, analyzing 1M+ AI citations between them — quantified which on-page formats answer engines (AI Overviews, Gemini, ChatGPT, Perplexity) cite most. Only three formats clear a ~65% average citation rate: product/landing pages (68.5%), blog posts (~67%), and listicles (66%); comparison pages sit just behind (~63%) but win ChatGPT specifically. Why it matters: AI search is now a real discovery channel, and citations — being named as the source inside the AI answer — are starting to matter more than classic backlinks for visibility. The lever isn't 'write more,' it's structure what you have so a model can lift a clean, attributable chunk.
blocks.doIt: Pick one page that should be winning AI mentions and reformat it in ~30 minutes: (1) match the title to the query intent the engines reward — What is X, X vs Y, How to X, or Best X; (2) lead each section with a direct one-sentence answer, then expand (engines lift the first clean sentence); (3) convert dense prose into a list or comparison table — pre-chunked, easy to extract; (4) add original/first-party stats (your own benchmark or survey number) — proprietary data is what gets attributed to you; (5) add an FAQ block with FAQ schema, a visible last-updated date, and an author bio. Then check whether ChatGPT/Perplexity cite you for the target question, and iterate.
blocks.note: Match format to the query's intent, the strongest predictor in the Wix data: informational questions cite articles ~2.7× more, while commercial ('best…', comparison) questions cite listicles for ~40% of citations. AEO isn't a separate site — it's a formatting pass on pages you already have; start with your highest-intent money pages, not the blog archive.

sources: Cloudflare — AI Gateway spend limits · Cloudflare Changelog — Control AI costs with spend limits · CNBC — Asia tech sell-off, SoftBank down · CNBC — Broadcom Q2 2026 earnings · Helion — $465M Series G · Axios — Helion $15.5B valuation · HubSpot — Content formats answer engines favor [research] · Wix Studio AI Search Lab — content types most cited by LLMs · HubSpot — Why citations matter more than backlinks for AI
